items.text: Earning a state MLO license is a gauntlet, and it's designed that way — the whole point of the SAFE Act was to make sure the people originating loans are trustworthy and competent. A candidate has to pass a background check, clear a set of minimum standards, complete required education, pass a national exam, and get sponsored by a licensed employer. Miss any one and the license doesn't issue.

It starts with the NMLS looking hard at who you are. Before applying, a candidate submits fingerprints for a state and national criminal-history check run through the FBI, authorizes the NMLS to pull an independent credit report, and provides a full personal history including any past administrative, civil, or criminal findings. The background check is the gate everything else sits behind.

Clearing the background check means meeting fixed standards. You must have never had an MLO license revoked. You can't have a felony conviction within the past 7 years — and for a felony involving fraud, dishonesty, a breach of trust, or money laundering, the bar is ever, with no time limit at all. Put a number on the general rule: a disqualifying felony conviction within the last 7 years blocks a license. You also have to show financial responsibility — no outstanding judgments (medical excepted) or tax liens, no foreclosures in the past 3 years, and no pattern of seriously delinquent accounts.

A license isn't usable on its own — it has to be tied to an employer. To originate, an MLO must be sponsored by a state-licensed entity. Submit your application without a sponsor and, if you're otherwise qualified, the state issues the license in an "approved-inactive" status. You can't originate a single loan until a sponsorship flips it to "approved-active." The license exists; the sponsorship is what switches it on.

Before the exam comes the classroom. A candidate must complete at least 20 hours of NMLS-approved pre-licensing education, and the hours are carved up: at least 3 hours of federal law, 3 hours of ethics (covering fraud, consumer protection, and fair lending), and 2 hours on non-traditional mortgage products. One approved course satisfies the national requirement for every state, though a particular state may pile its own state-specific hours on top.

The finish line is a national exam: a 120-question test written by the NMLS, and passing means scoring at least 75%. Fail and you can retake it twice more — three attempts in all — with each try at least 30 days after the last. Fail all three and you're locked out for 6 months before the cycle starts over. One more rule reaches into the future: let your license lapse for 5 years or more (not counting time you spent as a registered originator) and you have to sit the exam again.
